name: child-process
context: Spawn child processes, execute shell commands, and orchestrate external tools from Node.js
info: The Node.js child_process module provides four methods for running external programs: exec runs a command string through a shell and buffers stdout/stderr, execFile invokes a binary directly without a shell for better security and performance, spawn streams stdio for long-running or large-output processes, and fork creates a Node.js child with a built-in IPC channel for message passing. Each method has a synchronous variant (execSync, execFileSync, spawnSync) that blocks the event loop. All methods accept options for cwd, env, shell, timeout, maxBuffer, and signal (AbortController). The spawn function returns a ChildProcess object whose stdin, stdout, and stderr are Node streams — pipe them, inherit them, or ignore them via the stdio option. Exit codes indicate success (0) or failure (non-zero); signals like SIGTERM and SIGKILL indicate forced termination. Agents use child_process to run build tools, test runners, git commands, package managers, linters, formatters, and any CLI tool programmatically from JavaScript.
triggers: run a shell command from node
execute a build tool from code

sections.title: exec and execFile
sections.body: The `exec` function runs a command string through a shell (`/bin/sh` on Unix, `cmd.exe` on Windows) and buffers stdout and stderr into strings. Promisify it with `util.promisify` for async/await. Set `maxBuffer` to increase the 1MB default for commands with large output. Set `timeout` to kill runaway processes. `execFile` invokes a binary directly without a shell — arguments are passed as an array, preventing shell injection. Prefer execFile when you have a known binary and arguments. Both have sync variants (`execSync`, `execFileSync`) that block the event loop and should only be used in CLI scripts or startup code.

sections.title: spawn for Streaming
sections.body: The `spawn` function starts a process and returns a ChildProcess object with streaming stdin, stdout, and stderr. Use `stdio: 'inherit'` to forward all I/O to the parent terminal (preserves colors and interactivity). Use `stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe']` to capture output while ignoring stdin. Spawn does not buffer output, making it suitable for long-running processes and large outputs. The `shell: true` option runs the command through a shell, enabling wildcards and pipes but introducing injection risk. Use AbortController via the `signal` option for cancellation and timeouts.

sections.title: fork and IPC Messaging
sections.body: The `fork` function is a specialized spawn that creates a new Node.js process with an IPC channel. The parent calls `child.send(msg)` and listens with `child.on('message', handler)`. The child uses `process.send(msg)` and `process.on('message', handler)`. Messages are serialized via the structured clone algorithm (supports objects, arrays, Buffers, but not functions or classes). Fork is ideal for CPU-intensive work that would block the event loop — offload parsing, transformation, or computation to a forked worker. Each forked process is a full Node.js instance with its own V8 heap and event loop.

sections.title: Exit Codes, Signals, and Errors
sections.body: A process terminates with an exit code (0 = success, non-zero = failure) or a signal (SIGTERM, SIGKILL, SIGINT). The `close` event fires when all stdio streams are closed and provides both code and signal. The `error` event fires when the process cannot be spawned at all — typically ENOENT (binary not found) or EACCES (permission denied). Always handle both `error` and `close` events. Use AbortController with the `signal` option for timeout-based cancellation. Send SIGTERM for graceful shutdown and SIGKILL as a last resort. Check `child.killed` to determine if the parent sent the kill signal.

sections.title: Security and Shell Injection
sections.body: Shell injection occurs when untrusted input is interpolated into an `exec` command string. For example, `exec('git log ' + userInput)` allows arbitrary command execution if userInput contains shell metacharacters like `; rm -rf /`. Prevention: use `execFile` or `spawn` with arguments as an array — these bypass the shell entirely. If you must use `exec` (for shell features like pipes or globbing), sanitize input rigorously. Never pass user-controlled data to `exec` without validation. Prefer `spawn('git', ['log', userRef])` over `exec('git log ' + userRef)`. On Windows, be aware that `cmd.exe` has different metacharacters than `/bin/sh`.

scripts.name: Run Command
scripts.description: Execute shell commands with configurable exec or spawn mode, timeout, and environment

args.description: Arguments to pass to the command

cwd.description: Working directory for the child process

env.description: Environment variables for the child process

signal.description: Signal that terminated the process (e.g., SIGTERM, SIGKILL), null if not signaled
